chapter_name: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties

concepts.name: Dobereiner's Triads
concepts.explanation: Dobereiner (1829) grouped elements into triads of three elements each, where the atomic weight of the middle element is roughly the average of the other two, and its properties are intermediate.
concepts.formula_reaction: Atomic weight of middle ≈ (Atomic weight of first + Atomic weight of third) / 2. Example: Li (7), Na (23), K (39).

concepts.name: Newlands' Law of Octaves
concepts.explanation: Newlands (1865) arranged elements in increasing atomic weight and found that every eighth element had properties similar to the first, similar to the octaves in music.
concepts.formula_reaction: e.g., Li (1st) and Na (8th) have similar properties.

concepts.name: Mendeleev's Periodic Law and Table
concepts.explanation: Mendeleev (1869) stated that the properties of elements are a periodic function of their atomic weights. He arranged elements in horizontal rows (periods) and vertical columns (groups), leaving gaps for undiscovered elements (Eka-aluminium, Eka-silicon) and predicting their properties.
concepts.formula_reaction: Law: Properties of elements are a periodic function of their atomic weights.

concepts.name: Modern Periodic Law
concepts.explanation: Moseley (1913) established that atomic number (nuclear charge) is a more fundamental property than atomic mass. The modern periodic law states that the physical and chemical properties of elements are periodic functions of their atomic numbers.
concepts.formula_reaction: Law: Properties of elements are a periodic function of their atomic numbers.

concepts.name: IUPAC Nomenclature for Elements (Z > 100)
concepts.explanation: For temporary naming of newly discovered elements, IUPAC uses a systematic nomenclature based on numerical roots for the digits of the atomic number, followed by 'ium'. The symbol consists of the first letters of the roots.

concepts.name: Periods and Groups in the Modern Periodic Table
concepts.explanation: The long form of the periodic table has 7 horizontal rows called periods, corresponding to the principal quantum number (n), and 18 vertical columns called groups (numbered 1 to 18). Elements in the same group have similar outer electronic configurations.

concepts.name: Electronic Configurations in Periods
concepts.explanation: The period number indicates the highest principal quantum number (n) of the valence shell. The number of elements in a period equals the number of electrons required to fill the available atomic orbitals (2, 8, 8, 18, 18, 32 for periods 1 to 6).

concepts.name: s-Block Elements
concepts.explanation: Elements of Group 1 (alkali metals) and Group 2 (alkaline earth metals) have their outermost electrons in the s-orbital (ns¹ and ns² respectively). They are soft, highly reactive metals with low ionization enthalpies and predominantly form ionic compounds.

concepts.name: p-Block Elements
concepts.explanation: Elements of Groups 13 to 18 (excluding He) have their outermost electrons in the p-orbital (ns²np¹ to ns²np⁶). They include metals, non-metals, and metalloids. Group 18 (noble gases) have completely filled ns²np⁶ configuration and are highly stable.

concepts.name: d-Block Elements (Transition Elements)
concepts.explanation: Elements of Groups 3 to 12 have their last electron entering the d-orbital (general configuration: (n-1)d¹⁻¹⁰ ns⁰⁻²). They are all metals, exhibit variable oxidation states, form coloured ions, and are often paramagnetic and catalytic.

concepts.name: f-Block Elements (Inner-Transition Elements)
concepts.explanation: The two rows placed below the main table (Lanthanoids: Ce to Lu, and Actinoids: Th to Lr) have their last electron entering the f-orbital. They have configuration (n-2)f¹⁻¹⁴ (n-1)d⁰⁻¹ ns². Actinoids are radioactive.

concepts.name: Metals, Non-metals and Metalloids
concepts.explanation: Metals (left side, >78%) are solids (except Hg), good conductors, malleable, and ductile. Non-metals (top right) are gases or brittle solids with low conductivity. Elements along the zig-zag line (Si, Ge, As, Sb, Te) are metalloids with intermediate properties.

concepts.name: Atomic Radius (Covalent and Metallic Radius)
concepts.explanation: Atomic radius is half the internuclear distance between two identical atoms in a covalent bond (covalent radius) or in a metallic crystal (metallic radius). It decreases across a period due to increasing effective nuclear charge, and increases down a group due to the addition of new shells.

concepts.name: Ionic Radius and Isoelectronic Species
concepts.explanation: Cations are smaller than their parent atoms (loss of electron, reduced repulsion), while anions are larger (gain of electron, increased repulsion). For isoelectronic species (same number of electrons), the ionic radius decreases with increasing nuclear charge (higher Z gives smaller radius).

concepts.name: Ionization Enthalpy (ΔᵢH)
concepts.explanation: Ionization enthalpy is the energy required to remove the most loosely bound electron from an isolated gaseous atom in its ground state. It generally increases across a period (increasing nuclear charge) and decreases down a group (increasing size and shielding). Anomalies: Be > B (2s penetration > 2p), N > O (half-filled p³ stability).
concepts.formula_reaction: X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻; ΔᵢH is always positive.

concepts.name: Electron Gain Enthalpy (ΔₑgH)
concepts.explanation: Electron gain enthalpy is the enthalpy change when an electron is added to an isolated gaseous atom to form an anion. It is generally negative (exothermic) for non-metals. It becomes more negative across a period, but Cl has more negative value than F due to less electron-electron repulsion in the larger 3p orbital.
concepts.formula_reaction: X(g) + e⁻ → X⁻(g); ΔₑgH is negative for most non-metals.

concepts.name: Electronegativity (Pauling Scale)
concepts.explanation: Electronegativity is a qualitative measure of the ability of an atom in a chemical compound to attract shared electrons towards itself. On the Pauling scale, Fluorine is assigned the highest value (4.0). It increases across a period and decreases down a group.

concepts.name: Valence and Oxidation States
concepts.explanation: Valence for representative elements is either equal to the number of valence electrons (group number) or eight minus the group number. Oxidation state is the charge assigned based on electronegativity. E.g., in OF₂, O is +2; in Na₂O, O is -2.

concepts.name: Anomalous Properties of Second Period Elements
concepts.explanation: The first element of each group (Li, Be, B to F) shows anomalous behavior compared to its group members due to its small size, high charge/radius ratio, high electronegativity, and the absence of d-orbitals (limiting covalency to 4).

concepts.name: Diagonal Relationship
concepts.explanation: The first element of a group often resembles the second element of the next group (e.g., Li with Mg, Be with Al, B with Si). This is due to comparable ionic sizes and charge densities, leading to similar polarizing powers.

concepts.name: Periodic Trends in Chemical Reactivity and Oxides
concepts.explanation: Chemical reactivity is highest at the extremes of a period. Metallic character (and basicity of oxides) decreases left to right, while non-metallic character (and acidity of oxides) increases. Basic oxides (Na₂O) form bases with water; acidic oxides (Cl₂O₇) form acids; amphoteric oxides (Al₂O₃) react with both.
